trade.summary: Malaysia and Singapore share one of the most intensive bilateral trade relationships in the world relative to their sizes. Singapore is Malaysia's single largest trading partner. Deep supply chain integration means many goods cross the Johor-Singapore Causeway multiple times during production. Singapore serves as Malaysia's financial and logistics hub; Malaysia is Singapore's largest water supplier and important hinterland for manufacturing. Electronics, machinery, chemicals, and refined petroleum dominate trade flows.
disputes: Water pricing agreement renegotiation (ongoing longstanding issue)
Airspace management dispute over Johor airspace
Maritime boundary in Johor Strait periodically contested

diplomatic.summary: The Malaysia-Singapore relationship is one of the most complex in Southeast Asia — deeply intertwined economically yet historically fraught. The two countries share a common history (Singapore was part of Malaysia 1963-1965), ethnic Chinese majority in Singapore versus Malay majority in Malaysia, and numerous unresolved bilateral disputes. The 2019 maritime and airspace disputes caused significant friction but both sides have strong incentives to maintain stable ties. The Johor-Singapore Rapid Transit System project represents a new era of connectivity investment. The relationship is best described as 'friendly rivals' with deep economic codependency.
recent_incidents: Johor Bahru-Singapore RTS Link under construction
Periodic water pricing renegotiation demands from Malaysia

military.summary: No formal alliance but extensive practical security cooperation. Both countries coordinate on maritime security in the Strait of Malacca under the Malacca Straits Patrol. Five Power Defence Arrangements (FPDA) includes both nations along with Australia, UK, and New Zealand. Historical sensitivities around Singapore's defense posture vis-a-vis Malaysia mean formal military ties are carefully managed.

financial.summary: Singapore is overwhelmingly the largest source of FDI into Malaysia and the primary financial services hub for Malaysian corporations. GIC and Temasek hold significant Malaysian equity stakes. Malaysian banks (Maybank, CIMB) are major players in Singapore. The Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone is deepening financial integration. Cross-border investment is the lifeblood of both economies.

energy.summary: Malaysia is Singapore's primary water supplier under a 1962 Water Agreement. Malaysia exports natural gas to Singapore via pipeline. Singapore refines Malaysian and imported crude oil and re-exports refined products back to Malaysia. Energy interdependence is substantial.

scientific.summary: Strong academic ties. Many Malaysians (particularly ethnic Chinese) study in Singapore universities. NUS and NTU collaborate extensively with Malaysian universities. Joint research in biomedical sciences, engineering, and tropical medicine.
